Think of \(A\) acting on every sample point at once — a uniform operation applied to a whole dataset.


Frame the matrix as a verb / machine from the very first slide. Input vector, output vector. The diagonal scaling example is the gentlest possible: each axis stretched independently. Stress “the same matrix is applied to every sample identically” — that is what makes it a transformation of the whole dataset, e.g. a normalisation or a coordinate change.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
How to multiply \(A\mathbf{x}\) by hand
Each output entry is the dot product of a matrix row with \(\mathbf{x}\):
\[\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} ax + by \\ cx + dy \end{pmatrix}\]
Worked example: \[\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1\cdot3 + 2\cdot4 \\ 0\cdot3 + 1\cdot4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\]

Row 1 of \(A\) dotted with \(\mathbf{x}\) → first output entry.
Row 2 of \(A\) dotted with \(\mathbf{x}\) → second output entry.


This connects directly to Day 3: the matrix-vector product is literally a stack of dot products, one per row. Make students compute the (11, 4) example by hand on the spot. The “row dot vector” recipe is the single most important manual skill from this block for the written exam.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
The same product, columns view
There is a second, equally important way to read \(A\mathbf{x}\): as a linear combination of the columns of \(A\), weighted by the entries of \(\mathbf{x}\).
\[\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = 3\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + 4\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\]

“3 units of column 1, plus 4 units of column 2.”
This view explains why the output can only ever be a blend of the columns — the column space.


The columns view is conceptually deeper than the rows view and pays off later (column space, rank, PCA loadings). Same answer (11,4), different story: the input vector’s entries are mixing weights for the columns. Both views are worth memorising; rows are easier for hand computation, columns are better for understanding what outputs are reachable.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Matrix–vector product in R
A <- matrix(c(1, 2,
              0, 1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
x <- c(3, 4)
A %*% x
#      [,1]
# [1,]   11
# [2,]    4

%*% is matrix multiplication. Plain * would be elementwise — wrong here.
byrow = TRUE fills the matrix row by row, so it matches how we write it on paper. (Default is column by column!)


The single most common beginner mistake: using * instead of %*%. Hammer this. Also flag the byrow trap: matrix() fills columns first by default, which silently transposes your matrix relative to what you wrote on paper. Recommend byrow = TRUE while learning, and always print the matrix to confirm.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
The identity matrix — “do nothing”
\[I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad I\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} \ \text{for every } \mathbf{x}\]
The identity leaves every vector unchanged — the matrix version of multiplying by 1.
I <- diag(2)      # 2x2 identity
I
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    1    0
# [2,]    0    1
I %*% c(3, 4)     # 3 4  (unchanged)

The identity is the reference point for all transformations: it does nothing. diag(2) builds it in R. Later (eigenvalues, solving systems) the identity is everywhere, so introduce it now while it is trivial. Mention that diag() with a vector argument instead makes a scaling matrix — the next slide.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Scaling matrices
A diagonal matrix stretches each axis independently:
\[S = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} \ \Rightarrow\ \text{double the x-axis, halve the y-axis}\]
S <- diag(c(2, 0.5))
pts <- matrix(c(1, 1,
                2, 0,
                0, 3), nrow = 2)   # 3 points as COLUMNS
S %*% pts
#      [,1] [,2] [,3]
# [1,]    2    4  0.0    # x doubled
# [2,]  0.5    0  1.5    # y halved

Putting samples in columns lets one %*% transform the whole dataset at once.
Biology: this is exactly a per-axis normalisation / rescaling of features.


Scaling is the most intuitive non-trivial transformation. The key practical pattern is on this slide: store your samples as columns, then a single S %*% data transforms every sample simultaneously. Connect to normalisation — rescaling each feature (gene) by a constant is a diagonal matrix multiply. Watch the dimensions: S is 2x2, data is 2x3, output is 2x3.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Rotation matrices
To rotate every point by angle \(\theta\) (counter-clockwise) about the origin:
\[R(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}\]
theta <- pi/2                       # 90 degrees
R <- matrix(c(cos(theta), -sin(theta),
              sin(theta),  cos(theta)), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
round(R %*% c(1, 0))                # 0 1  -> x-axis rotates onto y-axis

A rotation changes direction but never length (it is rigid).
The columns of \(R\) are where the old axes land — a recurring theme.


Rotation introduces a transformation that mixes the axes (off-diagonal entries). The check round(R %*% c(1,0)) == c(0,1) makes the geometry tangible: the x-axis swings up onto the y-axis under a 90-degree turn. Emphasise that rotations preserve length and angles — useful intuition for later when we talk about orthogonal matrices and PCA’s rotation of the data cloud.

title: Matrices as transformations
section: Reading the columns of a transformation
text:
Reading the columns of a transformation
The columns of any matrix \(A\) tell you where the standard axes go:
\[A = \begin{pmatrix} \color{red}{a} & \color{blue}{b} \\ \color{red}{c} & \color{blue}{d} \end{pmatrix}\]

\(A\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix} = \color{red}{(a,c)}\) → first column = image of the x-axis unit vector.
\(A\begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix} = \color{blue}{(b,d)}\) → second column = image of the y-axis unit vector.

To design a transformation: decide where the axes should land, then write those as the columns.

This is a powerful shortcut and a frequent exam question: to understand or build a matrix, just ask where the unit axes go. Multiplying A by (1,0) selects column 1; by (0,1) selects column 2. This reframes “what does this matrix do?” as “look at its columns”, and reframes “build a matrix that does X” as “place the target axis images in the columns”.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Composing transformations = multiplying matrices
Do \(B\), then \(A\). Applied to \(\mathbf{x}\): first \(B\mathbf{x}\), then \(A(B\mathbf{x}) = (AB)\mathbf{x}\).
So composition is matrix multiplication, and \(AB\) means “apply \(B\) first, then \(A\)” — right to left.
\[AB:\quad (AB)_{ij} = (\text{row } i \text{ of } A)\cdot(\text{column } j \text{ of } B)\]
A <- matrix(c(2,0, 0,1), 2, byrow = TRUE)   # scale x by 2
B <- matrix(c(0,-1, 1,0), 2, byrow = TRUE)  # rotate 90 deg
A %*% B          # rotate THEN scale
B %*% A          # scale THEN rotate -- NOT the same!

Order matters: \(AB \neq BA\) in general. (Try both above.)


Composition is the reason matrix multiplication is defined the way it is — each entry is a row-dot-column, the Day 3 dot product again. The crucial cautionary point: matrix multiplication is NOT commutative, so the ORDER of operations matters, and AB applies the rightmost matrix first. Demonstrate A%%B vs B%%A giving different results live.

title: Matrices as transformations
section: Putting it together: transform a small dataset
text:
Putting it together: transform a small dataset
# 4 sample points (2 features each) stored as COLUMNS
data <- matrix(c( 1, 0,
                  0, 1,
                 -1, 0,
                  0,-1), nrow = 2)

theta <- pi/4
R <- matrix(c(cos(theta), -sin(theta),
              sin(theta),  cos(theta)), 2, byrow = TRUE)
S <- diag(c(2, 1))

out <- S %*% (R %*% data)   # rotate 45 deg, then stretch x by 2
round(out, 2)

plot(t(data), xlim=c(-3,3), ylim=c(-3,3), pch=19)   # before (rows = points)
points(t(out), col = "red", pch = 19)               # after
One %*% chain applies the whole pipeline to every sample at once.

This capstone block is what students should reproduce and stare at: a rotation then a scaling applied to a little square of points, plotted before (black) and after (red). Note the transpose t() in plot() because plot expects points as ROWS but we stored them as columns for the math. This “columns for math, transpose for plotting” pattern recurs throughout the course.

title: Matrices as transformations
section: Design / contrast matrices: a biology aside
text:
Design / contrast matrices: a biology aside
In differential-expression analysis (e.g. limma, DESeq2) a design matrix \(X\) encodes which samples are control vs treated. The model is
\[\mathbf{y} \approx X\boldsymbol\beta\]

\(X\) is a matrix that maps coefficients \(\boldsymbol\beta\) (e.g. baseline, treatment effect) to predicted expression \(\mathbf{y}\).
Same machinery as today: a matrix takes one vector and produces another, as a linear combination of its columns.

You will not build these by hand in this course — but recognise that the matrix-as-transformation idea is exactly what powers the statistics you already use.

This slide grounds the abstraction in tools the students likely already touch. A design matrix is just a transformation mapping effect sizes to expected measurements; fitting the model is “solving” that relationship (Days 1-2). The aim is recognition, not mastery: when they next see model.matrix() output, they should think “ah, a transformation, columns are the predictors”.

diag(2)


Scaling
diagonal matrix stretches each axis
diag(c(2, 0.5))


Rotation
rigid; preserves length
matrix(c(cos,-sin,sin,cos),2,byrow=TRUE)


Composition
apply \(B\) then \(A\) = \(AB\) (order matters!)
A %*% B



Next (Day 5): some special vectors are only scaled by a transformation — eigenvectors.

The summary table is the cheat sheet: everything is “row dot vector” under the hood. Remind them: %% not , watch byrow, store samples as columns. Preview Day 5: for most matrices a vector changes both length and direction, but special directions get only stretched (not rotated) — those eigenvectors reveal the deep structure of a transformation, and underlie PCA.

title: Quiz: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Short questions to test your geometric intuition. Try each on paper first, then open the answer. Several are written in the style of the on-campus exam (no computer).

Q1. A gene-expression profile of 12 000 genes is a vector. In how many dimensions does it “live”?






Hint





The dimension equals the number of components (measurements).









Answer





12 000 dimensions — one axis per gene. We cannot draw it, but every rule from 2-D and 3-D still applies.




Q2. Compute \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + 3\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\).






Hint





Scale first, then add componentwise.









Answer





\(3(1,2) = (3,6)\), then \((2,-1)+(3,6) = (5,\ 5)\).




Q3. What is the length of \(\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}\)?






Hint





\(\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2}\).









Answer





\(\sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10\).




Q4. Compute the dot product \(\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}\cdot\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix}\).






Hint





Multiply matching components and add.









Answer





\(1\cdot4 + 3\cdot0 + (-2)\cdot5 = 4 + 0 - 10 = -6\).




Q5. The dot product of two non-zero vectors is negative. What does that tell you about the angle between them?






Hint





Recall \(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = \|\mathbf{u}\|\|\mathbf{v}\|\cos\theta\), and lengths are positive.









Answer





Since the lengths are positive, a negative dot product forces \(\cos\theta < 0\), so the angle is greater than \(90^\circ\) — the vectors point in broadly opposite directions.




Q6. Are \(\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\) orthogonal?






Hint





Orthogonal iff the dot product is exactly 0.









Answer





\(3(-1) + 1\cdot3 = -3 + 3 = 0\) → yes, orthogonal (a right angle).




Q7. Two samples have profiles \(\mathbf{u}\) and \(\mathbf{v} = 5\mathbf{u}\). What is their cosine similarity, and why is that biologically reassuring?






Hint





\(\mathbf{v}\) is just \(\mathbf{u}\) scaled. Does scaling change direction?









Answer





Cosine similarity = 1. Scaling does not change direction, so two profiles that differ only by overall magnitude (e.g. sequencing depth) are correctly judged identical in shape. Cosine similarity is depth-invariant.




Q8. True or false: the dot product of two vectors is itself a vector.






Hint





Count the numbers in the result.









Answer





False. The dot product is a single scalar (one number).




Q9. Project \(\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\) onto \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\).






Hint





\(\text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u} = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\,\mathbf{v}\). Here \(\mathbf{v}\) is the x-axis.









Answer





\(\dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}} = \dfrac{5}{1} = 5\), so the projection is \(5(1,0) = (5,\ 0)\) — the shadow on the x-axis. (Makes sense: projecting onto the x-axis just keeps the x-component.)




Q10. After projecting \(\mathbf{u}\) onto \(\mathbf{v}\), what is special about the leftover \(\mathbf{u} - \text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u}\)?






Hint





Think about the right angle a shadow makes with the ground.









Answer





It is orthogonal to \(\mathbf{v}\) (dot product 0). The projection captures the part of \(\mathbf{u}\) along \(\mathbf{v}\); the leftover is the part with no overlap with \(\mathbf{v}\). This is the seed of PCA and least-squares regression.




Q11. Which R expression computes the dot product of vectors u and v? (a) u * v (b) sum(u * v) (c) u %o% v






Hint





A dot product returns a single number. * is elementwise; %o% is the outer product.









Answer






sum(u * v). Option (a) gives the elementwise products (a vector), and %o% gives an outer-product matrix. u %*% v also works, returning a \(1\times1\) matrix.





Q12. A sample \(\mathbf{u}\) has cosine similarity 0 with sample \(\mathbf{v}\). Interpret this.






Hint





\(\cos\theta = 0\) means what angle?









Answer





The profiles are orthogonal (\(\theta = 90^\circ\)): as different in direction as possible without pointing opposite. The two samples share no common expression pattern in this geometric sense.

title: Exercise: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
By the end of this exercise you should be able to, by hand and in R:

add and scale vectors,
compute a vector’s length (norm),
compute a dot product and read off its meaning,
compute the angle / cosine similarity between two vectors,
decide whether two vectors are orthogonal,
project one vector onto another and find the orthogonal leftover.

The by-hand problems are the ones that matter most for the written exam — the on-campus exam has no computer. Do them with pen and paper first, then check with R.

title: Exercise: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Background
A vector is an ordered list of numbers \(\mathbf{v} = (v_1, \dots, v_n)\). For us, a vector is a sample: a gene-expression profile across \(n\) genes.







Operation
Definition




Addition
\(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = (u_1 + v_1,\ \dots,\ u_n + v_n)\)


Scaling
\(c\,\mathbf{v} = (c v_1,\ \dots,\ c v_n)\)


Norm (length)
\(\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + \dots + v_n^2} = \sqrt{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\)


Dot product
\(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = u_1 v_1 + \dots + u_n v_n\)


Angle
\(\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\|\,\|\mathbf{v}\|}\)


Orthogonal
\(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = 0\)


Projection of \(\mathbf{u}\) onto \(\mathbf{v}\)
\(\text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u} = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\,\mathbf{v}\)



The geometric identity that ties dot product to angle: \[\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = \|\mathbf{u}\|\,\|\mathbf{v}\|\cos\theta.\]

title: Exercise: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
By hand
Use these vectors throughout (think of them as 2-gene mini-profiles): \[\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{c} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{d} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}\]
B1. Compute \(\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{c}\) and \(2\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}\).
B2. Compute the lengths \(\|\mathbf{a}\|\) and \(\|\mathbf{b}\|\). (Both are nice round numbers.)
B3. Compute the dot products \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}\) and \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{c}\).
B4. Using B2 and B3, compute \(\cos\theta\) for the angle between \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\). What is \(\theta\) in degrees?
B5. Show that \(\mathbf{b}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix}\) are orthogonal. Then find a number \(k\) so that \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ k \end{pmatrix}\) is orthogonal to \(\mathbf{a}\).
B6. Compare \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{d}\): compute \(\cos\theta\) between them. What do you notice, and what does it say about the two “samples”? (Hint: look at \(\mathbf{d}\) versus \(\mathbf{a}\) componentwise.)
B7. Project \(\mathbf{a}\) onto \(\mathbf{b}\), i.e. compute \(\text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}\mathbf{a}\). Then compute the leftover \(\mathbf{a} - \text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}\mathbf{a}\) and verify it is orthogonal to \(\mathbf{b}\).

title: Exercise: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
In R
Define the vectors and verify your by-hand answers, then scale up to a realistic-sized profile.
a <- c(3, 4)
b <- c(4, 0)
cc <- c(-2, 6)   # 'c' is a reserved function name in R, so use cc
d <- c(6, 8)

# B1 — addition and scaling
a + cc
2*b - a

# B2 — norms
sqrt(sum(a^2))   # = 5
sqrt(sum(b^2))   # = 4

# B3 — dot products
sum(a * b)
sum(a * cc)

# B4 — angle between a and b
cos_ab <- sum(a*b) / (sqrt(sum(a^2)) * sqrt(sum(b^2)))
cos_ab
acos(cos_ab) * 180/pi   # angle in degrees

# B5 — orthogonality check
sum(b * c(0, 5))        # should be 0

# B6 — a vs d
sum(a*d) / (sqrt(sum(a^2)) * sqrt(sum(d^2)))

# B7 — projection of a onto b, and the leftover
proj_ab <- (sum(a*b) / sum(b*b)) * b
proj_ab
leftover <- a - proj_ab
leftover
sum(leftover * b)       # should be 0 (orthogonal)

Scale up: comparing real-ish profiles
Reusable helper functions, then a small “expression matrix” of samples in columns:
norm_vec <- function(v) sqrt(sum(v^2))
cos_sim  <- function(u, v) sum(u*v) / (norm_vec(u) * norm_vec(v))

set.seed(1)
genes <- 200
# two samples of the same "cell type": s2 is s1 at double depth plus a little noise
s1 <- rpois(genes, lambda = 5)
s2 <- 2 * s1 + rpois(genes, lambda = 1)
# an unrelated sample
s3 <- rpois(genes, lambda = 5)

cos_sim(s1, s2)   # close to 1: same direction despite different depth
cos_sim(s1, s3)   # lower: unrelated profiles

# Compare with Euclidean distance, which IS fooled by depth:
norm_vec(s1 - s2)   # large, because s2 is ~twice as big
norm_vec(s1 - s3)   # smaller, even though s3 is unrelated!
R1. Run the block above. Explain in one sentence why cosine similarity ranks s1–s2 as most similar but Euclidean distance does not.
R2. Build a cosine-similarity matrix for all three samples. (Hint: stack them as columns of a matrix and use crossprod, or just call cos_sim on each pair.)

title: Exercise: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Hints






Hint 1 — building and combining vectors





Use c to build a vector. Plain +, - and * between a scalar and a vector, or between two equal-length vectors, work elementwise — exactly matching the by-hand definitions of addition and scaling.









Hint 2 — norm and dot product





The norm is sqrt(sum(v^2)). The dot product of u and v is sum(u*v); equivalently u %*% v (see %*%), which returns a \(1\times1\) matrix. Note \(\|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v} =\) sum(v*v).









Hint 3 — angles in R





R’s trig functions work in radians. Get the angle with acos and convert to degrees by multiplying by 180/pi. Clamp arguments that drift slightly outside \([-1,1]\) from rounding if needed.









Hint 4 — orthogonality





Two vectors are orthogonal iff their dot product is exactly 0. For B5, set \(\mathbf{a}\cdot(2, k) = 0\) and solve the resulting linear equation for \(k\).









Hint 5 — projection





First compute the scalar coefficient \(\dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\) = sum(u*v)/sum(v*v), then multiply it by the vector v. The leftover u - proj should dot to 0 with v.

title: Exercise: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Solutions






Solution





B1. \(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{c} = (3+(-2),\ 4+6) = (1,\ 10)\). \(2\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a} = (8,0)-(3,4) = (5,\ -4)\).
B2. \(\|\mathbf{a}\| = \sqrt{3^2+4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5\). \(\|\mathbf{b}\| = \sqrt{4^2+0^2} = 4\).
B3. \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b} = 3\cdot4 + 4\cdot0 = 12\). \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{c} = 3(-2) + 4\cdot6 = -6 + 24 = 18\).
B4. \(\cos\theta = \dfrac{12}{5\cdot4} = \dfrac{12}{20} = 0.6\). So \(\theta = \arccos(0.6) \approx 53.13^\circ\).
B5. \(\mathbf{b}\cdot(0,5) = 4\cdot0 + 0\cdot5 = 0\) → orthogonal. For \((2,k)\): \(\mathbf{a}\cdot(2,k) = 3\cdot2 + 4k = 6 + 4k = 0 \Rightarrow k = -\tfrac{3}{2}\).
B6. Note \(\mathbf{d} = (6,8) = 2\mathbf{a}\). So \(\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{a}\cdot 2\mathbf{a}}{\|\mathbf{a}\|\,\|2\mathbf{a}\|} = \dfrac{2\|\mathbf{a}\|^2}{2\|\mathbf{a}\|^2} = 1\), i.e. \(\theta = 0^\circ\). \(\mathbf{d}\) points in the same direction as \(\mathbf{a}\) — same “shape” of profile, just twice the magnitude (like the same cell type sequenced at double depth). Cosine similarity is blind to that scaling.
B7. Coefficient \(= \dfrac{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{12}{16} = \tfrac{3}{4}\). So \(\text{proj}_{\mathbf{b}}\mathbf{a} = \tfrac{3}{4}(4,0) = (3,\ 0)\). Leftover \(= \mathbf{a} - (3,0) = (0,\ 4)\). Check: \((0,4)\cdot(4,0) = 0\) ✓ (orthogonal, as expected — the projection drops a perpendicular onto the line through \(\mathbf{b}\), here the x-axis).
R answers (selected output):
a + cc            # 1 10
2*b - a           # 5 -4
sqrt(sum(a^2))    # 5
sum(a*b)          # 12
cos_ab            # 0.6
acos(0.6)*180/pi  # 53.13010
proj_ab           # 3 0
leftover          # 0 4
sum(leftover*b)   # 0
R1. s2 is essentially 2 * s1, i.e. the same direction in gene space; cosine similarity measures direction and so reports ~1, whereas Euclidean distance measures absolute separation and is inflated by the doubled magnitude.
R2. One compact way:
M <- cbind(s1, s2, s3)        # genes in rows, samples in columns
# normalise each column to unit length, then cross products give cosines
U <- apply(M, 2, function(v) v / norm_vec(v))
round(crossprod(U), 3)        # 3x3 cosine-similarity matrix
The s1–s2 entry is near 1; the entries involving s3 are lower.

title: Exercise: vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Where this leads

Day 4 turns the dot product into a building block: every entry of a matrix–vector product is a dot product of a matrix row with the vector. A matrix becomes a machine that transforms whole vectors.
Cosine similarity is the standard way to compare cells/samples in single-cell analysis (Week 5).
Projection is the engine of PCA (Week 2): a PCA score is the coordinate of a sample projected onto a principal axis, and the orthogonal-leftover idea is how successive components are made independent.

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
After this exercise you will be able to:

Build a coefficient matrix \(A\) and right-hand side \(\mathbf{b}\) in R and solve \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\) with solve.
Verify a solution with matrix multiplication %*%.
Recognise, by hand and in R, which of the three solution spaces a system has — one, none, or infinitely many — and tie that to whether a deconvolution problem is exactly-, over-, or under-determined.
Find a best-fit answer for an over-determined system with qr.solve / lm.

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
After this exercise you will be able to:

Build a coefficient matrix \(A\) and right-hand side \(\mathbf{b}\) in R and solve \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\) with solve.
Verify a solution with matrix multiplication %*%.
Recognise, by hand and in R, which of the three solution spaces a system has — one, none, or infinitely many — and tie that to whether a deconvolution problem is exactly-, over-, or under-determined.
Find a best-fit answer for an over-determined system with qr.solve / lm.

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
Background
A bulk expression sample is a mixture of cell types. With known signatures (columns of \(A\)) and a measured bulk profile \(\mathbf{b}\), the cell-type amounts \(\mathbf{x}\) satisfy \[
A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}.
\]
The shape of \(A\) tells you a lot. Let \(A\) have \(m\) rows (genes / equations) and \(n\) columns (cell types / unknowns):

\(m = n\), \(A\) non-singular \(\to\) exactly one solution (well-determined).
\(m > n\), equations conflict \(\to\) no exact solution (over-determined). Use least squares for the closest fit.
\(m < n\), or \(A\) singular \(\to\) infinitely many solutions (under-determined).

Two key facts:

\(A\) (square) is singular when one equation is a combination of the others — it carries no new information. Then \(\det A = 0\).
The rank of \(A\) is the number of genuinely independent equations. Full rank (\(=n\)) with a square \(A\) is exactly the unique-solution case.

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
By hand
Decide the solution space before running any R. For a \(2\times 2\) system, each equation is a line in the \((x_1, x_2)\) plane; the solutions are where the lines meet.

Problem 1 — classify three systems
For each, state whether it has one, none, or infinitely many solutions, and explain in one sentence (lines crossing / parallel / identical).

\(\;x_1 + x_2 = 4,\quad x_1 - x_2 = 0\)
\(\;x_1 + x_2 = 4,\quad 2x_1 + 2x_2 = 7\)
\(\;x_1 + x_2 = 4,\quad 3x_1 + 3x_2 = 12\)



Problem 2 — which deconvolution scenario?
Match each situation to one solution / no solution / infinitely many, and to well- / over- / under-determined:

You measure 5 marker genes to resolve 3 cell types, data is exact and consistent.
You measure 2 marker genes to resolve 3 cell types.
You measure 3 marker genes to resolve 3 cell types, signatures all distinct and independent.

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
In R

Step 1 — solve a well-determined system
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(8, 9)

x <- solve(A, b)
x
# expected: 3 2

A %*% x          # should reproduce b = c(8, 9)


Step 2 — confirm your by-hand classifications
Run each system from Problem 1 through solve and read the result or the error message.
# (a) one solution
solve(matrix(c(1, 1, 1, -1), 2, byrow = TRUE), c(4, 0))

# (b) no solution (inconsistent, singular A)
solve(matrix(c(1, 1, 2, 2), 2, byrow = TRUE), c(4, 7))
# Error: ... system is exactly singular ...

# (c) infinitely many (consistent, singular A)
solve(matrix(c(1, 1, 3, 3), 2, byrow = TRUE), c(4, 12))
# Error: ... system is exactly singular ...
Note that solve gives the same “singular” error for (b) and (c): it cannot tell “no solution” from “infinitely many”. Use the diagnostics below to distinguish them.


Step 3 — diagnose without solving
A_bc <- matrix(c(1, 1, 2, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
det(A_bc)        # ~0  -> singular, so NOT a unique-solution system
qr(A_bc)$rank    # 1   -> only one independent equation (of 2 unknowns)
Use det, qr$rank, and dim to characterise a system before trying to solve it.


Step 4 — an over-determined system and least squares
Five genes, two cell types, noisy data: no exact solution, so find the best fit.
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 3,
              1, 1,
              3, 2,
              0, 2), nrow = 5, byrow = TRUE)   # 5 genes, 2 cell types
b <- c(8.1, 8.9, 5.2, 10.8, 4.1)               # noisy measurements

# solve(A, b) would error: A is not square. Use least squares:
xhat <- qr.solve(A, b)
xhat

# Same answer via linear regression (no intercept):
coef(lm(b ~ A - 1))

# How far off is each equation? (the residuals)
A %*% xhat - b
We return to least squares and why it is the right notion of “closest” when we cover projection (Day 3) and regression later.

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
Hints






Hint 1 — building the matrix





Use matrix with byrow = TRUE so the numbers read in the same row order as the equations. Print the matrix and check it against the equations before solving.









Hint 2 — reading the lines (Problem 1)





Two lines either cross once (one solution), are parallel and distinct (no solution), or are the same line (infinitely many). Compare the coefficient ratios to the right-hand-side ratio: e.g. in (b) the left sides differ by a factor 2 but the right sides (\(4\) vs \(7\)) do not.









Hint 3 — what solve’s error means





solve errors with “system is exactly singular” for both no-solution and infinitely-many cases, and with “‘a’ must be a square matrix” for any non-square \(A\). The error tells you the system is not well-determined, but not which of the two singular cases it is.









Hint 4 — distinguishing none vs infinitely many





Both have singular \(A\). Check consistency: does \(\mathbf{b}\) lie in the column space? A practical check for these small cases is whether the proportionality of the rows of \(A\) is also matched by \(\mathbf{b}\). In Problem 1, (b) breaks it (\(4 \to 7\) is not \(\times 2\)) so no solution; (c) preserves it (\(4 \to 12\) is \(\times 3\)) so infinitely many.









Hint 5 — least squares functions





For an over-determined (tall, non-square) \(A\), use qr.solve(A, b) or fit a no-intercept regression with lm(b ~ A - 1) and read coef. The - 1 drops the intercept so the model is purely \(A\mathbf{x}\).

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
Solutions






Solution






Problem 1

One solution. Adding the equations: \(2x_1 = 4 \Rightarrow x_1 = 2\), then \(x_2 = 2\). The two lines cross once. (Solution \(x_1 = x_2 = 2\).)
No solution. Left sides are proportional (\(2\times\)) but right sides are not (\(4 \times 2 = 8 \ne 7\)). The lines are parallel and distinct — they never meet.
Infinitely many. The second equation is exactly \(3\times\) the first (including the right side: \(3 \times 4 = 12\)). It is the same line; every point on \(x_1 + x_2 = 4\) is a solution.



Problem 2

5 genes, 3 cell types, consistent \(\to\) over-determined by shape but consistent, so it happens to have one solution here (with exact data the extra equations agree). With noise it would have no exact solution and need least squares.
2 genes, 3 cell types \(\to\) under-determined, infinitely many solutions — too few markers to resolve the cell types.
3 genes, 3 cell types, independent \(\to\) well-determined, one solution.



R
# Step 1
A <- matrix(c(2, 1, 1, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
solve(A, c(8, 9))           # 3 2
A %*% c(3, 2)               # 8 9  (check)

# Step 2 (a) one solution
solve(matrix(c(1, 1, 1, -1), 2, byrow = TRUE), c(4, 0))   # 2 2
# (b) and (c) both throw "system is exactly singular"

# Step 3 diagnostics
A_bc <- matrix(c(1, 1, 2, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
det(A_bc)        # 0
qr(A_bc)$rank    # 1   (< 2 unknowns -> not unique)

# Step 4 least squares (numbers vary slightly with the noisy b)
A5 <- matrix(c(2,1, 1,3, 1,1, 3,2, 0,2), nrow = 5, byrow = TRUE)
b5 <- c(8.1, 8.9, 5.2, 10.8, 4.1)
qr.solve(A5, b5)            # e.g. ~ 2.97 2.03
coef(lm(b5 ~ A5 - 1))       # same values
A5 %*% qr.solve(A5, b5) - b5  # small, non-zero residuals
The residuals in Step 4 are not zero — that is the signature of an over-determined system: no exact fit exists, and least squares returns the closest compromise.

title: Exercise: Linear systems in R and solution spaces
text:
Where this leads

Day 3 explains why least squares is the right “closest” answer, through the geometry of vectors, dot products, and projection.
The full treatment of regression and constrained (non-negative) deconvolution comes later in the course; today’s unconstrained solve/lm is the stepping-stone, not the production tool.

title: Exercise: Fitting HMMs in R to find CpG islands
text:
Background
We reuse the Day-9 model. States \(\{H, L\}\): H = “GC-rich (CpG-island-like)”, L = “normal background”. Symbols are the bases \(\{A, C, G, T\}\).
\[
\boldsymbol{\pi}=\begin{bmatrix}0.5\\0.5\end{bmatrix},\quad
A=\begin{bmatrix}0.8&0.2\\0.3&0.7\end{bmatrix}\ (HL\times HL),\quad
B=\begin{bmatrix}0.1&0.4&0.4&0.1\\0.3&0.2&0.2&0.3\end{bmatrix}\ (ACGT).
\]
The HMM package maps one function onto each Day-9 idea: initHMM builds the model, simHMM generates data, forward gives the likelihood, viterbi decodes the best path, and baumWelch learns the parameters. Install it once with install.packages("HMM").

title: Exercise: Fitting HMMs in R to find CpG islands
text:
Setup
install.packages("HMM")   # run once
library(HMM)

states  <- c("H", "L")
symbols <- c("A", "C", "G", "T")

transProbs <- matrix(c(0.8, 0.2,
                       0.3, 0.7), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                     dimnames = list(states, states))

emissionProbs <- matrix(c(0.1, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1,
                          0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                        dimnames = list(states, symbols))

hmm <- initHMM(States = states, Symbols = symbols,
               startProbs = c(0.5, 0.5),
               transProbs = transProbs,
               emissionProbs = emissionProbs)
hmm   # inspect: check rows of transProbs and emissionProbs each sum to 1

title: Exercise: Fitting HMMs in R to find CpG islands
text:
In R

Task 1 — simulate and inspect
set.seed(1)
sim <- simHMM(hmm, length = 200)

obs   <- sim$observation     # the bases you "see"
truth <- sim$states          # the TRUE hidden path (hidden in real life)

head(obs,   20)
head(truth, 20)

# Q1a: what fraction of the simulated bases are G or C overall?
mean(obs %in% c("G", "C"))

# Q1b: among positions whose TRUE state is H, what fraction are G or C?
#      compare with the L positions. Does it match the emission matrix?
mean(obs[truth == "H"] %in% c("G", "C"))
mean(obs[truth == "L"] %in% c("G", "C"))


Task 2 — likelihood with forward
f <- forward(hmm, obs)        # log forward probabilities, states x positions
dim(f)                        # 2 x 200

# total log-likelihood = log-sum-exp of the final column
logsumexp <- function(v) { m <- max(v); m + log(sum(exp(v - m))) }
ll <- logsumexp(f[, length(obs)])
ll
# Q2: this is a LOG probability. Why is the raw probability P(obs) so tiny
#     that we must work in logs? (Recall how fast your Day-9 products shrank.)


Task 3 — decode the islands with viterbi
path <- viterbi(hmm, obs)

# Q3a: decoding accuracy against the known truth
mean(path == truth)

# Q3b: read off predicted CpG islands as maximal runs of "H"
runs <- rle(path)
runs

# turn runs into island coordinates (start, end, length) for the H runs
ends   <- cumsum(runs$lengths)
starts <- ends - runs$lengths + 1
islands <- data.frame(state = runs$values, start = starts, end = ends,
                      length = runs$lengths)
islands[islands$state == "H", ]
# Q3c: how many predicted islands are there, and how long is the longest?


Task 4 — learn the parameters with baumWelch
Now pretend you never knew \(A\) and \(B\). Learn them from obs alone, starting from a deliberately uninformative guess.
guess <- initHMM(states, symbols,
                 startProbs    = c(0.5, 0.5),
                 transProbs    = matrix(c(0.6, 0.4,
                                          0.4, 0.6), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                        dimnames = list(states, states)),
                 emissionProbs = matrix(rep(0.25, 8), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                        dimnames = list(states, symbols)))

bw <- baumWelch(guess, obs, maxIterations = 100)

round(bw$hmm$transProbs,    2)   # learned A
round(bw$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)   # learned B
# Q4a: which learned state ended up GC-heavy? Is it called "H" or "L"?
#      (This is LABEL SWITCHING — the name is arbitrary.)
# Q4b: decode with the LEARNED model and compare accuracy to Task 3.
path_learned <- viterbi(bw$hmm, obs)
mean(path_learned == truth)


Task 5 — local optima (a pitfall)
# Try a different starting guess and see if baumWelch lands somewhere else.
guess2 <- initHMM(states, symbols,
                  startProbs    = c(0.5, 0.5),
                  transProbs    = matrix(c(0.9, 0.1,
                                           0.1, 0.9), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                         dimnames = list(states, states)),
                  emissionProbs = matrix(c(0.4, 0.1, 0.1, 0.4,
                                           0.1, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                         dimnames = list(states, symbols)))
bw2 <- baumWelch(guess2, obs, maxIterations = 100)
round(bw2$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)
# Q5: do bw and bw2 agree (up to label switching)? What does this tell you
#     about trusting a single baumWelch run?

title: Exercise: Fitting HMMs in R to find CpG islands
text:
Hints






Hint 1 — building the model





initHMM takes States, Symbols, startProbs, transProbs, emissionProbs. Use byrow = TRUE and dimnames so the matrices read like the page, and double-check every row sums to 1 — a row summing to 0.99 is a silent bug. See the package reference manual.









Hint 2 — simHMM returns two things





simHMM returns a list: $observation (the emitted symbols, your data) and $states (the true hidden path). You only get $states because it is a simulation; on real data the states are unknown. Use set.seed() so your run is reproducible.









Hint 3 — forward gives LOG probabilities





forward returns a matrix of log forward probabilities (rows = states, columns = positions). The data log-likelihood is the log-sum-exp of the last column. Working in logs avoids numerical underflow — the same reason your Day-9 products got tiny so fast.









Hint 4 — reading islands off the path





viterbi returns one state label per position. rle (run-length encoding) collapses consecutive equal labels into runs; the runs whose value is "H" are your predicted CpG islands. cumsum(runs$lengths) gives each run’s end coordinate.









Hint 5 — learning and label switching





baumWelch returns $hmm (the fitted model) and $difference (convergence trace). The fitted state called "H" is not guaranteed to be the GC-rich one — decide which is the island state by reading bw$hmm$emissionProbs (the row with high C and G probability), not by its name. As an alternative package with more flexible emissions, see depmixS4.

title: Exercise: Fitting HMMs in R to find CpG islands
text:
Solutions






Solution






Task 1 — simulate and inspect
set.seed(1)
sim <- simHMM(hmm, length = 200)
obs   <- sim$observation
truth <- sim$states

mean(obs %in% c("G", "C"))
# [1] ~0.49        # overall GC content, a mix of both states

mean(obs[truth == "H"] %in% c("G", "C"))
# [1] ~0.80        # H emits C or G with prob 0.4 + 0.4 = 0.8
mean(obs[truth == "L"] %in% c("G", "C"))
# [1] ~0.40        # L emits C or G with prob 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4
Q1a/Q1b: the per-state GC fractions match the emission matrix (\(0.8\) for \(H\), \(0.4\) for \(L\)). The overall fraction sits in between, weighted by how much time the chain spends in each state.


Task 2 — likelihood
f  <- forward(hmm, obs)
logsumexp <- function(v) { m <- max(v); m + log(sum(exp(v - m))) }
ll <- logsumexp(f[, length(obs)])
ll
# [1] roughly -270   (a large negative log-probability)
Q2: \(P(\mathbf{x})\) is a product of ~200 emission and ~200 transition factors, each below 1, so it is around \(10^{-117}\) — far smaller than a double can represent. Working with the log keeps the number in a sane range; this is exactly why the by-hand products in Day 9 collapsed toward zero so quickly.


Task 3 — decoding
path <- viterbi(hmm, obs)
mean(path == truth)
# [1] ~0.88        # most positions decoded correctly; not 100% because
#                  # emissions are genuinely ambiguous

runs <- rle(path)
ends   <- cumsum(runs$lengths)
starts <- ends - runs$lengths + 1
islands <- data.frame(state = runs$values, start = starts, end = ends,
                      length = runs$lengths)
islands[islands$state == "H", ]
#   state start end length
# 1     H     1   7      7
# 5     H    33  41      9
# ...
Q3: decoding is good but imperfect because a single base never determines the state; Viterbi uses the surrounding context. The number and length of H runs are your predicted islands (your exact numbers depend on the seed).


Task 4 — learning
bw <- baumWelch(guess, obs, maxIterations = 100)
round(bw$hmm$transProbs, 2)
round(bw$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)
# one row of emissionProbs becomes GC-heavy (high C and G), the other flatter;
# transProbs drift toward sticky diagonals -- close to the true 0.8/0.7,
# allowing for finite-sample noise and possible label swap.

path_learned <- viterbi(bw$hmm, obs)
mean(path_learned == truth)
# [1] ~0.85        # close to the accuracy from the true model
Q4a: whichever learned row has high C+G probability is the island state, regardless of whether the package labelled it "H" or "L" — that is label switching. Q4b: decoding with the learned model recovers the truth almost as well as the true model, which is the point: we found the structure from unlabelled sequence.


Task 5 — local optima
bw2 <- baumWelch(guess2, obs, maxIterations = 100)
round(bw2$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)
Q5: the two runs usually find essentially the same solution up to label switching, but not always — EM only reaches a local optimum, so a poor start can land elsewhere. The lesson: run baumWelch from several initialisations, compare the resulting log-likelihoods (via forward), and keep the best — never trust a single run blindly.

title: Exercise: Fitting HMMs in R to find CpG islands
text:
Where this leads
You have run the full HMM workflow on genome-like data: build, simulate, score, decode into an island map, and learn parameters from unlabelled sequence — and you have seen the pitfalls (local optima, label switching, choosing the number of states). This closes the linear-algebra block.
The ideas continue through the course. In week 2, PCA replaces discrete hidden states with continuous latent directions found via the eigenvectors of days 5–6. The Bayesian-statistics block generalises today’s “infer the hidden from the observed” with priors and full posteriors. And single-cell analysis will segment and annotate cells much as you segmented and annotated a genome here. You now hold the complete linear-algebra toolkit the rest of the course rests on.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
About this lecture
Today we start the linear-algebra block. By the end you will be able to:

Recognise when a biology problem is a system of linear equations.
Write such a system in the standard form \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\).
Solve small \(2\times 2\) and \(3\times 3\) systems by hand using substitution and Gaussian elimination.
Check a candidate solution by plugging it back in.

No prior linear algebra is assumed. We build the ideas from a concrete example and keep all numbers small enough to do with pen and paper.

Welcome to day one of the linear-algebra block. This is the foundation for everything that follows — eigenvalues, Markov chains, PCA — so we go slowly. Today is deliberately by-hand and concept-heavy: the exam is written and on-campus, so you need to be able to solve a small system without a computer. Tomorrow we do the same thing in R and at scale.

title: Solving linear equation systems
section: A biology problem: what is in the mixture?
text:
A biology problem: what is in the mixture?
A tumour biopsy is not one cell type. It is a mixture: some tumour cells, some immune cells, some healthy tissue. Suppose we already know two marker readouts per cell type (say, expression of gene G1 and gene G2), measured per “unit” of each cell type:




Tumour
Immune




Gene G1 signal
2
1


Gene G2 signal
1
3



We sequence a bulk sample and measure total signal: G1 \(= 8\), G2 \(= 9\).
Question: how many units of tumour (\(x_1\)) and immune (\(x_2\)) cells were in the sample?

This is the motivating pairing for the whole first two days: cell-type deconvolution. A bulk measurement is a sum over the cell types present. If we know the per-cell-type signature, recovering the amounts is exactly solving a linear system. Keep the numbers tiny here on purpose — we will make them realistic in R tomorrow.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Turning the biology into equations
Each measured signal is the sum of contributions from each cell type:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\underbrace{2x_1 + 1x_2}_{\text{total G1}} &= 8\\[2pt]
\underbrace{1x_1 + 3x_2}_{\text{total G2}} &= 9
\end{aligned}
\]
Two unknowns (\(x_1, x_2\)), two equations. This is a system of linear equations.
“Linear” means every unknown appears only to the first power — no \(x_1^2\), no \(x_1 x_2\), no \(\sin x_1\). Just numbers times unknowns, added up.

Stress what “linear” rules out: no squares, no products of unknowns, no nonlinear functions. Each equation is a weighted sum of the unknowns set equal to a number. Almost every method in this course reduces, at its core, to solving or approximately solving a system like this.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
The standard form \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\)
We separate the known coefficients from the unknowns and the measurements:
\[
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}}_{A}
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{x}}
=
\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 9 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{b}}
\]

\(A\) — the coefficient matrix. Here, the cell-type signatures (one column per cell type, one row per gene).
\(\mathbf{x}\) — the unknowns we want (the cell-type amounts).
\(\mathbf{b}\) — the right-hand side, our measurements.

The first row of \(A\) times \(\mathbf{x}\) reproduces the first equation; the second row gives the second. This packaging is what lets us scale to hundreds of genes later.

Introduce the names carefully: coefficient matrix A, unknown vector x, right-hand side b. Notation conventions for the course: uppercase for matrices, bold lowercase for vectors. Walk through how “row of A times x” rebuilds each equation — this is the first glimpse of matrix-vector multiplication, which we formalise on day 4.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Strategy 1 — Substitution
Solve one equation for one unknown, then substitute into the other.
From equation 1: \(\;2x_1 + x_2 = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 8 - 2x_1\).
Put that into equation 2:
\[
x_1 + 3(8 - 2x_1) = 9 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 + 24 - 6x_1 = 9 \;\Rightarrow\; -5x_1 = -15
\]
So \(x_1 = 3\), and then \(x_2 = 8 - 2(3) = 2\).
Answer: 3 units of tumour, 2 units of immune cells.

Substitution is the method most students already half-remember from school. Do it slowly. Isolate one variable, push it into the other equation, you now have one equation in one unknown. Solve, then back-substitute. The arithmetic is the only place to slip up, so go step by step on the board.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Always check your answer
Plug \(x_1 = 3,\; x_2 = 2\) back into the original equations:
\[
\begin{aligned}
2(3) + 1(2) &= 6 + 2 = 8 \quad\checkmark\\
1(3) + 3(2) &= 3 + 6 = 9 \quad\checkmark
\end{aligned}
\]
Both hold, so the solution is correct.
Checking costs seconds and catches almost every arithmetic mistake. On the exam, always do it.

Make checking a habit and say so explicitly. Substituting back into the original equations is independent of how you got the answer, so it catches sign errors and arithmetic slips. This is free marks on an exam and free peace of mind in real analysis.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Strategy 2 — Gaussian elimination
Substitution gets messy with three or more unknowns. Gaussian elimination is the systematic method that scales (it is what R uses internally).
The idea: add multiples of one equation to another to eliminate a variable, until the system is easy to read off.
Two moves are allowed (they never change the solution):

Multiply an equation by a non-zero number.
Add a multiple of one equation to another.

(A third, swapping two equations, just reorders them.)

Frame Gaussian elimination as “substitution made systematic”. The allowed operations are the row operations; emphasise that each one preserves the solution set, which is why we are allowed to do them. This is the algorithm behind solve() in R, so understanding it by hand demystifies the computer.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Gaussian elimination on our 2×2
Start:
\[
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + 1x_2 &= 8 \quad(\text{R1})\\
1x_1 + 3x_2 &= 9 \quad(\text{R2})
\end{aligned}
\]
Eliminate \(x_1\) from R2. Replace R2 by \(\;\text{R2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\text{R1}\):
\[
\Big(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2\Big)x_1 + \Big(3 - \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1\Big)x_2
= 9 - \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 8
\;\Rightarrow\; 0\,x_1 + 2.5\,x_2 = 5
\]
Now R2 says \(2.5\,x_2 = 5 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2\). Back-substitute into R1: \(2x_1 + 2 = 8 \Rightarrow x_1 = 3\). Same answer as before. \(\checkmark\)

Show the multiplier choice: we want to kill x1 in R2, R2 has coefficient 1 and R1 has coefficient 2, so subtract half of R1. The x1 term vanishes by design. Then the last equation has a single unknown — solve it, then back-substitute upward. Reassure them it gives the same answer as substitution; it is the same maths, organised.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
The augmented matrix shorthand
We do not need to keep writing \(x_1, x_2\). Track only the numbers in an augmented matrix \([\,A \mid \mathbf{b}\,]\):
\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 2 & 1 & 8 \\ 1 & 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{\;\text{R2}\,-\,\frac12\text{R1}\;}
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 2 & 1 & 8 \\ 0 & 2.5 & 5 \end{array}\right]
\]
The bottom row reads \(2.5\,x_2 = 5\). The goal is this upper-triangular (“staircase”) shape: zeros below the diagonal. Then solve bottom-up.

The augmented matrix is bookkeeping: a vertical bar separates A from b, and we apply the same row operations to the whole row including the b entry. Target shape is upper triangular — once you are there, the last row is one equation in one unknown and you climb back up. This notation is essential for the 3x3 case.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Scaling up: a 3×3 system
Three cell types (tumour, immune, stroma), three marker genes. Signatures and measurements give:
\[
\begin{aligned}
1x_1 + 1x_2 + 1x_3 &= 6\\
0x_1 + 2x_2 + 1x_3 &= 7\\
2x_1 + 1x_2 + 1x_3 &= 8
\end{aligned}
\qquad
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & 6\\
0 & 2 & 1 & 7\\
2 & 1 & 1 & 8
\end{array}\right]
\]
We eliminate column by column, top-left to bottom-right.

Now the payoff for systematic elimination. With three unknowns substitution becomes error-prone, but elimination is just “clear column 1, then clear column 2”. The first equation conveniently already has a 1 in the top-left, our pivot. Walk through the plan before doing the arithmetic.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
3×3 elimination — clear column 1
Pivot is the \(1\) in the top-left. R2 already has a \(0\) there. Clear R3 with \(\;\text{R3} - 2\,\text{R1}\):
\[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & 6\\
0 & 2 & 1 & 7\\
2 & 1 & 1 & 8
\end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{\;\text{R3}-2\text{R1}\;}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & 6\\
0 & 2 & 1 & 7\\
0 & -1 & -1 & -4
\end{array}\right]
\]
Column 1 now has zeros below the pivot.

R3 minus two times R1: 2 minus 2 is 0, 1 minus 2 is minus 1, 1 minus 2 is minus 1, and 8 minus 12 is minus 4. Do each subtraction out loud. The point is that the first unknown is now confined to the first equation only.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
3×3 elimination — clear column 2
Pivot is now the \(2\) in R2. Clear R3 with \(\;\text{R3} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{R2}\):
\[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & 6\\
0 & 2 & 1 & 7\\
0 & -1 & -1 & -4
\end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{\;\text{R3}+\frac12\text{R2}\;}
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 1 & 1 & 6\\
0 & 2 & 1 & 7\\
0 & 0 & -0.5 & -0.5
\end{array}\right]
\]
Now upper-triangular. The bottom row reads \(-0.5\,x_3 = -0.5\).

R2 has minus 1 in column 2 below the pivot 2, so add half of R2 to R3 to kill it. Minus 1 plus 1 is 0; minus 1 plus a half is minus a half; minus 4 plus 3.5 is minus a half. We have reached the staircase shape. Now we read off and back- substitute.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
3×3 — back-substitution
From the bottom up:
\[
\begin{aligned}
-0.5\,x_3 &= -0.5 &&\Rightarrow\; x_3 = 1\\
2x_2 + x_3 &= 7 &&\Rightarrow\; 2x_2 + 1 = 7 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 3\\
x_1 + x_2 + x_3 &= 6 &&\Rightarrow\; x_1 + 3 + 1 = 6 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 2
\end{aligned}
\]
Solution: \(x_1 = 2,\; x_2 = 3,\; x_3 = 1\).
Check in the original: \(2+3+1=6\,\checkmark\), \(0+6+1=7\,\checkmark\), \(4+3+1=8\,\checkmark\).

Back-substitution always starts at the last row, which has one unknown. Solve it, carry that value up into the second-last row which now has one remaining unknown, and so on. Then check all three original equations. This is the full recipe they need for the exam.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
The same idea in R (preview)
Tomorrow we let the computer do the elimination. The code is short:
A <- matrix(c(1, 1, 1,
              0, 2, 1,
              2, 1, 1), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(6, 7, 8)
solve(A, b)
# [1] 2 3 1
solve() runs Gaussian elimination internally — exactly what you just did by hand, but on any size.

A quick reassurance and a hook into day two. We will not type matrices by hand forever; solve does the elimination for us. But knowing what it does is what lets you trust it, debug it, and answer exam questions where no computer is allowed. Note byrow = TRUE so the matrix reads like the page.

title: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Summary

A linear system = weighted sums of unknowns set equal to numbers.
Standard form: \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\) (\(A\) coefficients, \(\mathbf{x}\) unknowns, \(\mathbf{b}\) measurements).
Substitution for tiny systems; Gaussian elimination (via the augmented matrix) for anything bigger — eliminate to upper-triangular, then back-substitute.
Always check by substituting back into the original equations.
Biology hook: recovering cell-type amounts from bulk measurements.

Next: Day 2 — the same in R, and what happens when there is no solution or infinitely many.

Recap the four takeaways and the biology framing. Flag the open question for tomorrow: so far every system has had exactly one answer, but that is not guaranteed — sometimes there is none, sometimes infinitely many, and that maps directly onto whether a deconvolution problem is well posed. Point them to the exercise and quiz.

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
After this exercise you will be able to:

Translate a small biology mixture problem into a system of linear equations and write it as \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\).
Solve \(2\times 2\) and \(3\times 3\) systems by hand using substitution and Gaussian elimination with an augmented matrix.
Check a solution by substituting it back into the original equations.
Reproduce your by-hand answers in R with solve.

This is the by-hand fluency you need for the written exam, so do the pen-and- paper parts before you touch R.

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
After this exercise you will be able to:

Translate a small biology mixture problem into a system of linear equations and write it as \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\).
Solve \(2\times 2\) and \(3\times 3\) systems by hand using substitution and Gaussian elimination with an augmented matrix.
