This is the by-hand fluency you need for the written exam, so do the pen-and- paper parts before you touch R.

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
Background
A bulk sample is a mixture of cell types. If we know, for each cell type, how much signal it contributes to each measured gene (its signature), then the total measured signal is a weighted sum of the unknown cell-type amounts. With as many genes measured as cell types present, recovering the amounts is exactly solving a square linear system.
In standard form, \[
A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b},
\] where the column \(j\) of \(A\) is the signature of cell type \(j\), the entry \(x_j\) is the unknown amount of cell type \(j\), and \(\mathbf{b}\) holds the bulk measurements. Row \(i\) of the system reads \[
A_{i1}x_1 + A_{i2}x_2 + \dots + A_{in}x_n = b_i,
\] the total signal for gene \(i\).
Two solution methods:

Substitution — solve one equation for one unknown and substitute into the others. Good for \(2\times 2\).
Gaussian elimination — use row operations (multiply a row by a non-zero number; add a multiple of one row to another) to reach upper-triangular form, then back-substitute from the bottom row up. Scales to any size.

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
By hand
Do these on paper. Show the row operations, not just the final numbers.

Problem 1 — a 2×2 mixture (substitution)
A blood sample is a mix of two cell types. Per unit, their signatures for genes G1 and G2 are:




Type A
Type B




G1
3
1


G2
1
2



The bulk measurements are G1 \(= 11\) and G2 \(= 7\).

Write the system as two equations and as \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\).
Solve for the amounts \(x_1\) (Type A) and \(x_2\) (Type B) by substitution.
Check your answer in both original equations.



Problem 2 — the same 2×2 by Gaussian elimination
Re-solve Problem 1 using Gaussian elimination on the augmented matrix \([\,A \mid \mathbf{b}\,]\). Reach upper-triangular form, then back-substitute. You should of course get the same answer as in Problem 1.


Problem 3 — a 3×3 mixture (Gaussian elimination)
Three cell types, three marker genes give the system \[
\begin{aligned}
1x_1 + 2x_2 + 1x_3 &= 8\\
2x_1 + 1x_2 + 1x_3 &= 7\\
1x_1 + 1x_2 + 3x_3 &= 10
\end{aligned}
\]

Write the augmented matrix \([\,A \mid \mathbf{b}\,]\).
Eliminate column 1, then column 2, to reach upper-triangular form.
Back-substitute to find \(x_1, x_2, x_3\), and check all three equations.

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
In R
Use R only to verify the answers you already found by hand.
# Problem 1 / 2 — the 2x2 system
A <- matrix(c(3, 1,
              1, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(11, 7)

x <- solve(A, b)   # solve A x = b
x
# expected: your Type A and Type B amounts from the by-hand answer

# Check: does A %*% x reproduce b?
A %*% x
# Problem 3 — the 3x3 system
A3 <- matrix(c(1, 2, 1,
               2, 1, 1,
               1, 1, 3), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
b3 <- c(8, 7, 10)

x3 <- solve(A3, b3)
x3

# Verify the residual is (numerically) zero
A3 %*% x3 - b3
Note byrow = TRUE: it fills the matrix row by row, so the R code reads in the same order as the equations on paper. (The default, byrow = FALSE, fills column by column — a common source of confusion.)

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
Hints






Hint 1 — setting up A and b





Each equation is one row. The numbers multiplying \(x_1, x_2, \dots\) go into \(A\) in order; the right-hand number goes into \(\mathbf{b}\). For Problem 1, the two equations are \(3x_1 + 1x_2 = 11\) and \(1x_1 + 2x_2 = 7\).









Hint 2 — substitution





From the second equation of Problem 1, \(x_1 = 7 - 2x_2\). Substitute that into the first equation to get a single equation in \(x_2\) alone, solve it, then back-substitute to get \(x_1\).









Hint 3 — choosing the elimination multiplier





To kill the entry in row \(i\), column \(j\) using a pivot row \(p\) (which has a non-zero entry in column \(j\)), use \[
\text{R}_i \;\leftarrow\; \text{R}_i - \frac{A_{ij}}{A_{pj}}\,\text{R}_p .
\] Apply the operation to the whole augmented row, including the \(\mathbf{b}\) entry.









Hint 4 — back-substitution





After reaching upper-triangular form, the last row has only one unknown — solve it. Carry that value into the row above, which now also has one remaining unknown, and continue upward.









Hint 5 — the R functions





matrix builds a matrix (use byrow = TRUE); solve with two arguments solves \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\); %*% is matrix multiplication, used here to check the answer.

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
Solutions






Solution






Problem 1 — substitution

Equations: \(3x_1 + x_2 = 11\) and \(x_1 + 2x_2 = 7\). In standard form, \[
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 11 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}.
\]
From equation 2, \(x_1 = 7 - 2x_2\). Substitute into equation 1: \[
3(7 - 2x_2) + x_2 = 11 \;\Rightarrow\; 21 - 6x_2 + x_2 = 11
\;\Rightarrow\; -5x_2 = -10 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 2.
\] Then \(x_1 = 7 - 2(2) = 3\). So \(x_1 = 3\) (Type A), \(x_2 = 2\) (Type B).
Check: \(3(3) + 1(2) = 11\ \checkmark\) and \(1(3) + 2(2) = 7\ \checkmark\).



Problem 2 — Gaussian elimination
\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 3 & 1 & 11 \\ 1 & 2 & 7 \end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{\;\text{R2}-\frac13\text{R1}\;}
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 3 & 1 & 11 \\ 0 & \tfrac{5}{3} & \tfrac{10}{3} \end{array}\right]
\] Bottom row: \(\tfrac{5}{3}x_2 = \tfrac{10}{3} \Rightarrow x_2 = 2\). Back-substitute into R1: \(3x_1 + 2 = 11 \Rightarrow x_1 = 3\). Same answer.


Problem 3 — 3×3 Gaussian elimination

Augmented matrix: \[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 8\\
2 & 1 & 1 & 7\\
1 & 1 & 3 & 10
\end{array}\right]
\]
Clear column 1 with \(\text{R2}-2\text{R1}\) and \(\text{R3}-\text{R1}\): \[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 8\\
0 & -3 & -1 & -9\\
0 & -1 & 2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
\] Clear column 2 with \(\text{R3} - \tfrac{1}{3}\text{R2}\) (since \(-1 = \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot(-3)\)): \[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 8\\
0 & -3 & -1 & -9\\
0 & 0 & \tfrac{7}{3} & 5
\end{array}\right]
\] (Row 3: \(2 - \tfrac13(-1) = \tfrac{7}{3}\) and \(2 - \tfrac13(-9) = 5\).)
Back-substitute: \[
\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{7}{3}x_3 &= 5 &&\Rightarrow\; x_3 = \tfrac{15}{7}\approx 2.143\\
-3x_2 - x_3 &= -9 &&\Rightarrow\; -3x_2 = -9 + \tfrac{15}{7} = -\tfrac{48}{7}
   \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = \tfrac{16}{7}\approx 2.286\\
x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 &= 8 &&\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 8 - \tfrac{32}{7} - \tfrac{15}{7}
   = 8 - \tfrac{47}{7} = \tfrac{9}{7}\approx 1.286
\end{aligned}
\] So \(x_1 = \tfrac{9}{7},\ x_2 = \tfrac{16}{7},\ x_3 = \tfrac{15}{7}\).

Check equation 1: \(\tfrac{9}{7} + 2\cdot\tfrac{16}{7} + \tfrac{15}{7}
= \tfrac{9 + 32 + 15}{7} = \tfrac{56}{7} = 8\ \checkmark\). (Equations 2 and 3 check out the same way.)


R verification
A <- matrix(c(3, 1, 1, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(11, 7)
solve(A, b)
# [1] 3 2

A3 <- matrix(c(1, 2, 1,
               2, 1, 1,
               1, 1, 3), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
b3 <- c(8, 7, 10)
solve(A3, b3)
# [1] 1.285714 2.285714 2.142857   (= 9/7, 16/7, 15/7)

A3 %*% solve(A3, b3) - b3
# all entries ~0 (tiny floating-point residual)

title: Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand
text:
Where this leads
Every system here had exactly one solution. That is not guaranteed: Day 2 builds these systems in R, and asks what happens when \(A\) is singular or non-square — the cases of no solution and infinitely many solutions, which correspond to a deconvolution problem being over- or under-determined.

title: Quiz: Solving linear equation systems
text:
Short questions to test your understanding of linear systems and solving them by hand. Try each before opening the hint or answer.

Q1. Which of these is not a linear equation in the unknowns \(x_1, x_2\)?

\(2x_1 + 5x_2 = 3\) (ii) \(x_1 - x_2 = 0\) (iii) \(x_1 x_2 = 4\)
\(7x_1 = 1\)







Hint





“Linear” means each unknown appears only to the first power, on its own — never squared, and never multiplied by another unknown.









Answer





(iii). The term \(x_1 x_2\) is a product of two unknowns, which is not linear. The others are all weighted sums of the unknowns.




Q2. Write the system \[
\begin{aligned}
4x_1 + x_2 &= 9\\
x_1 + 3x_2 &= 6
\end{aligned}
\] in the form \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\). What are \(A\) and \(\mathbf{b}\)?






Hint





Each equation is a row. The coefficients of \(x_1, x_2\) fill \(A\); the right-hand numbers fill \(\mathbf{b}\).









Answer





\[
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}.
\]




Q3. In the deconvolution setup \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\), what does each of \(A\), \(\mathbf{x}\), and \(\mathbf{b}\) represent biologically?






Hint





One holds known cell-type signatures, one the unknown amounts, one the bulk measurements.









Answer





\(A\) = the cell-type signatures (column \(j\) = signature of cell type \(j\)); \(\mathbf{x}\) = the unknown amounts of each cell type; \(\mathbf{b}\) = the measured bulk signals.




Q4. Solve by substitution: \(x_1 + x_2 = 5\) and \(x_1 - x_2 = 1\).






Hint





From the first equation, \(x_1 = 5 - x_2\). Substitute into the second.









Answer





\(x_1 = 5 - x_2\), so \((5 - x_2) - x_2 = 1 \Rightarrow 5 - 2x_2 = 1 \Rightarrow
x_2 = 2\), and \(x_1 = 3\). Check: \(3 + 2 = 5\ \checkmark\), \(3 - 2 = 1\ \checkmark\).




Q5. Which of the following row operations is not allowed during Gaussian elimination (because it would change the solution set)?

Multiply a row by \(0\). (ii) Multiply a row by \(-2\).
Add 3 times row 1 to row 2. (iv) Swap row 1 and row 2.







Hint





What information is lost if you multiply an entire equation by zero?









Answer





(i). Multiplying by zero turns an equation into \(0 = 0\), destroying its information. The allowed scaling is by any non-zero number.




Q6. You have reduced an augmented matrix to \[
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 1 & 2 & 5 \\ 0 & 3 & 9 \end{array}\right].
\] What are \(x_1\) and \(x_2\)?






Hint





Read the bottom row first — it has only one unknown.









Answer





Bottom row: \(3x_2 = 9 \Rightarrow x_2 = 3\). Top row: \(x_1 + 2(3) = 5 \Rightarrow x_1 = -1\).




Q7. Is \(x_1 = 2,\ x_2 = 1\) a solution of \(5x_1 - x_2 = 9\) and \(2x_1 + 3x_2 = 8\)? Show how you check.






Hint





Substitute both values into both original equations and see if each holds.









Answer





Eq 1: \(5(2) - 1 = 9\ \checkmark\). Eq 2: \(2(2) + 3(1) = 7 \ne 8\). The second equation fails, so no, it is not a solution.




Q8. To eliminate the \(x_1\) term from row 2, you have rows R1: \(2x_1 + 4x_2 = 6\) and R2: \(3x_1 + x_2 = 4\). What multiple of R1 do you subtract from R2?






Hint





You want the new \(x_1\) coefficient in R2 to be zero. The multiplier is \(A_{21}/A_{11}\).









Answer





\(\tfrac{3}{2}\): replace R2 by \(\text{R2} - \tfrac{3}{2}\text{R1}\). The \(x_1\) coefficient becomes \(3 - \tfrac{3}{2}\cdot 2 = 0\).




Q9. In R, what does solve(A, b) return, and what does A %*% x compute?






Hint





One solves the system; the other is matrix multiplication used to check it. See solve and %*%.









Answer





solve(A, b) returns the vector \(\mathbf{x}\) solving \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\). A %*% x multiplies \(A\) by \(\mathbf{x}\); if \(\mathbf{x}\) is correct it should reproduce \(\mathbf{b}\).




Q10. When you build a matrix in R with matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2), the result has \(1, 2\) in the first column, not the first row. What argument makes it fill by row instead, so the code matches the equations on paper?






Hint





See the byrow argument of matrix.









Answer





byrow = TRUE. By default matrix fills column by column (byrow = FALSE), which is a common source of mistakes when transcribing a system.




Q11. A bulk sample contains 3 cell types but you measured only 2 marker genes. Have you written enough equations to pin down all three amounts? Why?






Hint





Count equations versus unknowns. Each measured gene gives one equation; each cell type is one unknown.









Answer





No — 2 equations, 3 unknowns. The system is under-determined, so it generally has infinitely many solutions rather than one. (Day 2 makes this precise.)

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
About this lecture
This is the final day of the linear-algebra block. Yesterday you built HMMs by hand; today we let R do the heavy lifting on genome-like data. By the end you will be able to:

Build an HMM in R with the CRAN HMM package via initHMM (states, symbols, \(\boldsymbol{\pi}\), \(A\), \(B\)).
Simulate sequence with simHMM and recover its likelihood with forward.
Decode the most likely hidden-state path with viterbi and read it as a predicted CpG-island map.
Learn the parameters \(A\), \(B\), \(\boldsymbol{\pi}\) from unlabelled sequence with baumWelch.
Discuss how many states to use and the main pitfalls.

Notation as Day 9: transition matrix \(A\), emission matrix \(B\), initial distribution \(\boldsymbol{\pi}\), hidden states \(\mathbf{z}\), observations \(\mathbf{x}\).

Welcome to day ten, the last day of the block. Yesterday was all concept and pen-and-paper; today is hands-on R. We use the same two-state GC-rich versus normal model so nothing about the biology is new — only the tooling. The HMM package gives us one function per task: build, simulate, score, decode, and learn. Code on these slides is illustrative, not executable; the exercise is where you run it.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Recap and today’s tool
Day 9: an HMM = hidden states + transition matrix \(A\) + emission matrix \(B\) + initial distribution \(\boldsymbol{\pi}\), with three problems — likelihood (forward), decoding (Viterbi), learning (Baum–Welch).
The CRAN HMM package gives one function for each:



Task (Day 9)
HMM function




build the model
initHMM


generate data
simHMM


likelihood \(P(\mathbf{x})\)
forward (and backward)


decode best path \(\mathbf{z}^\*\)
viterbi


learn \(A, B, \boldsymbol{\pi}\)
baumWelch



install.packages("HMM")   # once
library(HMM)

Map each Day-9 concept onto exactly one function so the package feels like a direct translation of yesterday’s ideas. initHMM builds the four ingredients, simHMM runs the generative story, forward and backward give the likelihood, viterbi decodes, and baumWelch learns. Mention depmixS4 as a more flexible alternative for richer emission models, but we stick with HMM because it maps cleanly onto the concepts.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Building the model with initHMM
The same 2-state model as Day 9. States \(H\) (GC-rich), \(L\) (normal); symbols the bases.
library(HMM)

states  <- c("H", "L")
symbols <- c("A", "C", "G", "T")

startProbs <- c(0.5, 0.5)                    # pi

transProbs <- matrix(c(0.8, 0.2,             # A: rows = from, cols = to
                       0.3, 0.7),
                     nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                     dimnames = list(states, states))

emissionProbs <- matrix(c(0.1, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1,  # B: rows = state, cols = base
                          0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.3),
                        nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                        dimnames = list(states, symbols))

hmm <- initHMM(States = states, Symbols = symbols,
               startProbs = startProbs,
               transProbs = transProbs,
               emissionProbs = emissionProbs)
Each matrix row must sum to 1 — exactly the constraint from Day 9.

Type this slowly. The arguments to initHMM are the four ingredients by name: States and Symbols are the labels, startProbs is pi, transProbs is A, and emissionProbs is B. Use byrow equals TRUE so the matrices read like the page, and dimnames so rows and columns are labelled. Stress the row-sum-to-one constraint; a common bug is a typo that makes a row sum to 0.99 or 1.01.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Simulating sequence with simHMM
simHMM runs the generative story for you and, because it is a simulation, also hands back the true hidden states — a luxury you never get with real data, but perfect for testing.
set.seed(1)
sim <- simHMM(hmm, length = 30)

sim$observation   # the emitted bases  (this is your "data")
#  [1] "G" "C" "C" "G" "A" ...

sim$states        # the TRUE hidden path (normally unknown!)
#  [1] "H" "H" "H" "H" "L" ...
We will pretend we only saw sim$observation, then check whether decoding recovers sim$states.

simHMM is our test harness. It samples a hidden path from the chain and emits a base at each step, returning both. The observation vector is what we would get from a sequencer; the states vector is the ground truth that real biology hides from us. The plan is honest model evaluation: hide the truth, run the algorithms on the observations alone, then compare against the truth we secretly kept.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Likelihood with forward
forward returns a matrix of log forward probabilities, states × position. The likelihood \(P(\mathbf{x})\) is recovered from the last column.
obs <- sim$observation

f <- forward(hmm, obs)        # log alpha values, rows = states, cols = positions

# total log-likelihood = log-sum-exp of the final column
logLik <- function(col) {
  m <- max(col)
  m + log(sum(exp(col - m)))
}
logLik(f[, length(obs)])
# e.g.  -39.5      (a log-probability: the raw probability is astronomically tiny)
Probabilities of long sequences are vanishingly small, so the package works in logs — this is why your by-hand products from Day 9 shrank so fast.

forward gives the log alpha matrix. To get the data log-likelihood you combine the final column with a numerically safe log-sum-exp — subtract the max before exponentiating to avoid underflow. Connect this back to yesterday: when they multiplied six probabilities they already saw the number collapse toward zero; over thousands of bases it underflows entirely, which is exactly why everything is done in log space. They mainly need to know that forward gives likelihood and that it lives in logs.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
section: Decoding with viterbi: the island map
text:
Decoding with viterbi: the island map
viterbi returns the single most likely hidden path — one label per base.
path <- viterbi(hmm, obs)
path
#  [1] "H" "H" "H" "H" "L" "L" "L" "H" "H" ...

# How well did we recover the truth?
mean(path == sim$states)      # fraction of positions correctly decoded, e.g. 0.87

# Read off predicted CpG islands: maximal runs of "H"
rle(path)
# Run Length Encoding
#   lengths: int [1:5] 4 3 2 ...
#   values : chr [1:5] "H" "L" "H" ...
The runs of H are your predicted CpG islands. rle turns the path into segments.

This is the biological payoff. viterbi takes only the observations and returns the best hidden label per position. Compare to sim states to measure accuracy; it will not be perfect because the emissions are genuinely ambiguous, but it should be high. Then rle compresses the path into runs, and each run of H is a predicted island with a start, end, and length. This is a real genome annotation produced from raw sequence.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Learning with baumWelch
So far we supplied \(A, B, \boldsymbol{\pi}\). The realistic case: only raw sequence, no labels. baumWelch learns the parameters by EM.
# start from a deliberately WRONG guess to show it can learn
guess <- initHMM(states, symbols,
                 transProbs    = matrix(c(0.5,0.5, 0.5,0.5), 2, byrow=TRUE,
                                        dimnames=list(states,states)),
                 emissionProbs = matrix(rep(0.25, 8), 2, byrow=TRUE,
                                        dimnames=list(states,symbols)))

bw <- baumWelch(guess, obs, maxIterations = 100)

bw$hmm$transProbs      # learned A  -> should drift toward 0.8/0.2, 0.3/0.7
bw$hmm$emissionProbs   # learned B  -> H row should become GC-heavy
baumWelch returns $hmm (the fitted model) and $difference (convergence trace).

Baum-Welch is the learning step. We hand it a deliberately uninformative starting model — uniform transitions and uniform emissions — and let it iterate. With enough data it pulls the parameters toward the truth: the H row of the emission matrix becomes GC-heavy and the transitions become sticky. Point out the return structure: dollar hmm is the fitted model you then feed to viterbi, and dollar difference lets you check it converged. Then immediately raise the caveats on the next slide.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Pitfalls of learning
baumWelch is powerful but easy to misuse:

Local optima. EM climbs to the nearest peak, not the global one. Different starting guesses → different fits. Run several inits, keep the best likelihood.
Label switching. Which learned state is “H” is arbitrary — the algorithm may swap them. Interpret by the emission profile (the GC-heavy one is your island state), not by the name.
Needs enough data. Short sequences give unstable estimates.
Garbage in, garbage out. The fitted states are just clusters; they are “CpG islands” only if you check their emission profile makes biological sense.


Spend real time here; this is where students get burned. EM only finds a local optimum, so multiple restarts and picking the best log-likelihood is standard practice. Label switching trips everyone up: the state the package calls H need not be the GC-rich one, so always read the learned emission matrix to decide which state means island. More data stabilises estimates. And finally, an HMM will happily partition any sequence into states whether or not they mean anything biological — interpretation is on you.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
How many hidden states?
We assumed 2 states (island vs not). In general you must choose the number.

Let biology guide you first: island vs background is naturally 2; coding / intron / intergenic might want 3+.
More states always fit the training data better (higher likelihood) — so raw likelihood cannot pick the number. Penalise complexity with AIC/BIC, or use held-out sequence.
More states = more parameters = more data needed and more local optima.

Start simple, add states only if they earn their keep and stay interpretable.

Choosing the number of states is a model-selection problem, the same tension we will meet repeatedly later in the course. Biology gives the first guess. Warn them that likelihood always rises with more states, so you cannot choose by likelihood alone; you need a penalty like AIC or BIC, or validation on held-out data. And every extra state costs parameters, data, and stability. The mantra: start simple, justify any added complexity.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
From toy to real genomes
What changes when you move from simHMM data to a real chromosome:

Symbols. Use the 4 bases, or richer features (dinucleotides like CpG, or k-mers) for sharper islands.
Scale. Real sequences are megabases long; forward/viterbi are linear in length, so this is fine — but work in logs (the package does).
Validation. Compare decoded islands against annotated CpG islands (e.g. UCSC tracks) to judge the model.
Beyond 2 states. Gene-finding HMMs (e.g. GENSCAN-style) use many states for exons, introns, splice sites — same machinery, bigger \(A\) and \(B\).


Bridge from the toy to practice. The biggest real-world refinement is the emission alphabet: modelling dinucleotides captures CpG depletion far better than single bases. Reassure them that scale is not a problem because the algorithms are linear in sequence length. Stress validation against known annotations, and note that serious gene finders are just large HMMs with the identical forward, Viterbi, and Baum-Welch underneath.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Summary

The CRAN HMM package = one function per Day-9 concept: initHMM, simHMM, forward, viterbi, baumWelch.
Decode with viterbi; the runs of the GC-rich state are your predicted CpG islands.
Learn parameters from unlabelled sequence with baumWelch — but watch for local optima, label switching, and enough data.
Choosing the number of states is model selection: let biology lead, use AIC/BIC or held-out data, keep states interpretable.

That closes the linear-algebra block — from solving systems to chains and hidden chains.

Recap the function-per-concept mapping and the biological read-out of the decoded path. Re-emphasise the three pitfalls of learning and the model- selection nature of choosing the number of states. This is the end of the whole linear-algebra arc; the next slide points forward to where these ideas resurface in the rest of the course.

title: Fitting HMMs in R: finding CpG islands
text:
Where this leads
This is the end of the linear-algebra block. The thinking carries forward:

Week 2 — PCA & applications. From discrete hidden states to continuous latent directions: PCA finds the axes (eigenvectors!) that explain variation in omics data — the eigen-ideas from days 5–6 return.
Bayesian statistics. HMMs were our first latent-variable models with proper inference; the Bayesian block generalises “infer the hidden given the observed” with priors and full posteriors.
Single-cell analysis. Segmenting and annotating cells echoes today’s segmenting and annotating a genome.

You now have the full toolkit — linear systems, vectors, matrices, eigenvalues, Markov chains, and HMMs — that the rest of the course builds on.

End by pointing forward, not back. PCA is the natural next step: continuous latent structure found via eigenvectors, tying back to days five and six. The Bayesian block generalises today’s infer-the-hidden idea with priors and posteriors. And single-cell annotation rhymes with island annotation. Congratulate them — they have crossed the hardest block, and everything after this leans on it.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
About this lecture
Yesterday we solved small systems \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\) by hand. Today we hand the work to R — and discover that not every system has a tidy answer. You will be able to:

Build \(A\) and \(\mathbf{b}\) in R and solve with solve.
Recognise the three kinds of solution space: exactly one, none, or infinitely many.
Connect each kind to a deconvolution scenario: exactly-, over-, or under-determined.
Know what to reach for when \(A\) is singular or non-square: least squares, via lm (full treatment later).


Today is the R day. The key conceptual jump is that “solve a linear system” is not always a well-posed request — there may be no answer, or a whole family of answers. We tie that to whether a cell-type deconvolution problem has enough, too few, or conflicting measurements. Keep the code minimal and the intuition front and centre.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Recap: the deconvolution problem
A bulk expression sample is a mixture. Known per-cell-type signatures (matrix \(A\), one column per cell type) and the measured bulk profile (vector \(\mathbf{b}\)) give a system \[
A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b},
\] whose solution \(\mathbf{x}\) is the fractions (or amounts) of each cell type. Today: what if the system cannot be solved exactly?

Recall the running example. The number of measured genes is the number of equations (rows of A); the number of cell types is the number of unknowns (columns of A). The relationship between those two counts is what drives everything today.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Building \(A\) and \(\mathbf{b}\) in R
# Two cell types, two marker genes
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(8, 9)

A
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    2    1
# [2,]    1    3

matrix with byrow = TRUE fills row by row, so the code reads like the equations on paper.
nrow sets the number of rows; c(...) is just the entries.
Default byrow = FALSE fills column by column — a classic bug.


Spend a moment on byrow because it bites everyone once. The default fills down the first column, so a matrix typed left-to-right comes out transposed unless you set byrow = TRUE. Always print the matrix and eyeball it against your equations before trusting it.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Solving with solve()
x <- solve(A, b)
x
# [1] 3 2          # 3 units tumour, 2 units immune

# Always check: does A x reproduce b?
A %*% x
#      [,1]
# [1,]    8
# [2,]    9

solve(A, b) returns the \(\mathbf{x}\) with \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\) — it runs Gaussian elimination, exactly what you did by hand on day 1.
%*% is matrix multiplication. Use it to verify, just like substituting back on paper.


solve is the workhorse. Two arguments means “solve this system”. Note that it returns the same 3, 2 we found by hand yesterday. The verification step with the matrix product is the R equivalent of plugging back into the original equations — make it a reflex.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
solve(A) alone gives the inverse
Ainv <- solve(A)        # one argument: the inverse of A
Ainv %*% b
# [1] 3 2               # same answer, x = A^{-1} b

solve(A, b) is preferred over solve(A) %*% b: it is faster and more numerically accurate (it never forms the inverse explicitly).
The inverse \(A^{-1}\) only exists when \(A\) is square and non-singular — precisely the “exactly one solution” case below.


Worth showing so they recognise both forms in the wild, but steer them toward the two-argument solve. Forming the inverse is wasteful and less stable. Flag that the inverse existing is equivalent to the system having a unique solution — this links directly into the next slides.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Three kinds of solution space
For \(A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}\) there are exactly three possibilities:

Exactly one solution — the system is consistent and determined.
No solution — the equations contradict each other.
Infinitely many solutions — the equations are consistent but leave freedom.

The next slides take each in turn and pair it with a deconvolution scenario.

This trichotomy is a theorem: a linear system has zero, one, or infinitely many solutions — never, say, exactly two. Geometrically (day 3) each equation is a line or plane, and we are asking how they intersect. For now, anchor each case to the biology.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Case 1 — exactly one (well-determined)
As many independent measurements as unknowns. \(A\) is square and non-singular.
Biology: #genes measured = #cell types, and the signatures are genuinely different. The deconvolution has a single answer.
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
solve(A, c(8, 9))
# [1] 3 2          # the unique solution
This is the happy case from day 1. solve() just returns the answer.

This is the case students already understand from yesterday. Stress the two conditions: square (equations equal unknowns) and non-singular (the equations are genuinely independent, not rephrasings of each other). When both hold, there is one and only one solution and solve returns it.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Case 2 — no solution (over-determined)
More equations than unknowns, and they conflict. Often \(A\) is non-square (more rows than columns).
Biology: we measured more genes than cell types. With real noisy data the equations rarely agree exactly, so no \(\mathbf{x}\) fits all of them.
A <- matrix(c(1, 0,
              0, 1,
              1, 1), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)   # 3 eqns, 2 unknowns
b <- c(2, 3, 6)                                # but 2 + 3 = 5, not 6
solve(A, b)
# Error in solve.default(A, b) : 'a' must be a square matrix
solve() refuses: it only handles square systems.

Over-determined means too many equations. With exact data they might still all agree, but with measurement noise they generally do not — there is no x that satisfies every equation at once. solve flatly errors on a non-square A. The fix, least squares, is two slides away. Note this is actually the realistic omics case: thousands of genes, a handful of cell types.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Case 3 — infinitely many (under-determined)
Fewer independent equations than unknowns. \(A\) is singular (or has fewer rows than columns).
Biology: we measured too few genes to distinguish the cell types — many fraction combinations explain the data equally well.
A <- matrix(c(1, 1,
              2, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)   # row 2 = 2 x row 1
b <- c(3, 6)
solve(A, b)
# Error in solve.default(...) : Lapack routine dgesv: system is exactly singular
Both equations say the same thing (\(x_1 + x_2 = 3\)), so any \(\mathbf{x}\) on that line works. solve() errors on the singular matrix.

Under-determined means too few independent equations. Here row two is just twice row one — no new information — so the matrix is singular and there is a whole line of solutions. solve detects the singularity and stops. The biology: not enough markers to resolve the cell types. Contrast the two error messages on this and the previous slide so they can diagnose which case they hit.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
section: Detecting which case you are in
text:
Detecting which case you are in
dim(A)                 # rows (equations) vs cols (unknowns)
det(A)                 # square only: ~0 means singular
qr(A)$rank             # number of independent equations

dim non-square \(\Rightarrow\) over- or under-determined by shape.
det \(\approx 0\) (square) \(\Rightarrow\) singular \(\Rightarrow\) not Case 1.
qr$rank < #unknowns \(\Rightarrow\) the equations do not pin down every unknown.

A square \(A\) with \(\det A \ne 0\) and full rank is exactly Case 1.

Give them a diagnostic toolkit. dim tells you the shape. det near zero flags a singular square matrix — but careful, floating point means it is rarely exactly zero, so “small relative to the entries” is the real test. Rank is the most robust single number: full rank equal to the number of unknowns is the unique- solution condition.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
section: When there is no exact answer: least squares
text:
When there is no exact answer: least squares
For over-determined systems (Case 2) we cannot satisfy every equation, so we find the \(\mathbf{x}\) that comes closest — minimising the total squared mismatch \(\lVert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\rVert^2\). This is least squares.
A <- matrix(c(1, 0,
              0, 1,
              1, 1), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(2, 3, 6)

qr.solve(A, b)            # least-squares solution
# [1] 2.333333 3.333333

coef(lm(b ~ A - 1))       # same thing, via linear regression
qr.solve and lm both give the best-fit \(\mathbf{x}\). We treat this properly later in the course.

This is the bridge to the rest of the course. When you cannot fit everything, fit as well as possible by minimising squared error — that is least squares, and it is what lm does under the hood. Mention it lightly here; the geometry (projection, day 3) and the full regression treatment come later. The takeaway: an over-determined deconvolution is solved by least squares, not solve.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
A note for real deconvolution
Real cell-type fractions must be non-negative and usually sum to 1. Plain solve/lm know nothing about that and can return negative fractions.

Production tools (e.g. CIBERSORT, and Bioconductor packages) add these constraints (non-negative least squares, support-vector regression, etc.).
The linear-system core is the same — the constraints are extra structure on top.

We use the unconstrained version to learn the mechanics; the constrained version is the real tool.

Honesty about what we defer, per the course design principles. The bare linear algebra can hand you a negative fraction, which is biologically nonsense. Real deconvolution adds constraints. We deliberately strip those away to expose the linear-system skeleton students need to learn first. Name-drop the real tools so they know where to look.

title: Linear systems in R; solution spaces
text:
Summary

Build systems in R with matrix (byrow = TRUE!) and solve with solve(A, b); check with %*%.
A linear system has one, none, or infinitely many solutions — matching well-, over-, and under-determined deconvolution.
Diagnose with dim, det, qr$rank.
Over-determined \(\Rightarrow\) least squares (qr.solve, lm); full treatment later.

Next: Day 3 — the geometric picture: vectors, dot products, and projection (which is why least squares works).

Pull the threads together: the R mechanics, the three solution spaces tied to the biology, the diagnostics, and the least-squares escape hatch. Preview day 3, where the geometry explains the least-squares idea we waved at today. Point them to the exercise to practise all three cases in R.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
About this lecture
Today we stop solving systems and start seeing them. We build a geometric picture of the objects we have been pushing around.

A vector is both an arrow and a row of numbers — and a gene-expression profile is a vector.
Adding and scaling vectors.
The length (norm) of a vector.
The dot product: one number that measures how two vectors line up.
The dot product gives the angle between vectors → cosine similarity, the workhorse of comparing samples.
Orthogonality (a right angle = zero dot product).
Projection: the shadow of one vector on another.


This is the conceptual heart of the linear-algebra block. Days 1–2 were about manipulating equations; today we attach geometry to those numbers so that everything later (PCA, eigenvectors, Markov chains) has a mental picture behind it. Emphasise repeatedly: the same list of numbers is simultaneously an arrow in space and a measurement of a biological sample. There is a quiz for this day because the concepts are slippery; encourage students to do it.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
section: A gene-expression profile is a vector
text:
A gene-expression profile is a vector
Measure the expression of 3 genes in one tissue sample:
\[\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 0 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix} \quad \begin{array}{l}\text{(GeneA = 12)}\\\text{(GeneB = 0)}\\\text{(GeneC = 7)}\end{array}\]

A vector is just an ordered list of numbers. Here, 3 numbers → one point/arrow in 3-D space.
A real RNA-seq sample has ~20 000 genes → a vector in 20 000-dimensional space. We cannot draw it, but every rule we learn in 2-D and 3-D still holds.
Two ways to read the same object:

as data: “this sample’s measurements”
as an arrow: from the origin to the point \((12, 0, 7)\).



The single most important idea of the day, stated on slide 2: a sample IS a vector. Stress that nothing about the math cares whether the dimension is 2 or 20000 — we draw pictures in 2-D only because we have two eyes. Tell them that “dimension” just means “number of genes / number of measurements”. Keep coming back to this profile vector throughout.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Vectors as arrows
In 2-D, the vector \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\) is the arrow from the origin to the point \((3, 2)\).

The numbers are the components: move 3 right, 2 up.
An arrow has a direction and a length.
We can slide the arrow around, but by convention we draw it starting at the origin.

# In R a vector is the most basic object
v <- c(3, 2)
v
# [1] 3 2

Draw the arrow on the chalkboard or refer to the slide figure. The point: components ARE instructions for how far to move along each axis. Mention that physicists let arrows float anywhere, but for data we anchor them at the origin so each sample is one fixed point. Note c() builds a vector in R — same word, same idea.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Adding vectors = combining profiles
Add component by component:
\[\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}\]

Geometrically: place the tail of the second arrow at the head of the first (“tip-to-tail”).
Biological reading: if \(\mathbf{x}\) is a baseline profile and \(\mathbf{d}\) is the change after a drug, then \(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{d}\) is the treated profile.

c(3, 2) + c(1, 4)
# [1] 4 6

Vector addition is the tip-to-tail rule. The drug example makes it concrete: differences of expression vectors are themselves vectors, and adding them moves you to a new sample. R adds vectors elementwise with plain +, which matches the math exactly — no special function needed.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Scaling a vector
Multiply every component by a scalar:
\[2 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad -1 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}\]

A scalar \(> 1\) stretches the arrow; between 0 and 1 shrinks it; negative flips it.
Scaling never changes the direction (except the flip) — only the length.
Biological reading: doubling a library’s sequencing depth scales the whole count vector by ~2; normalisation is exactly choosing a scalar to make samples comparable.

2 * c(3, 2)
# [1] 6 4

Scaling stretches/shrinks along the same line. The normalisation point is important and previews later material: when we divide each sample by its total counts (or library size) we are rescaling each expression vector. Direction = “what genes are high relative to each other”; length = “overall magnitude / depth”.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Length of a vector (the norm)
The length (or norm) of \(\mathbf{v}\) comes straight from Pythagoras:
\[\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \dots + v_n^2}\]
For \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\): \(\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5\).
v <- c(3, 4)
sqrt(sum(v^2))   # 5
sqrt(sum(v*v))   # same thing

A vector of length 1 is a unit vector — pure direction, no magnitude.


The norm is just the straight-line distance from the origin to the point — Pythagoras in n dimensions. Students already know Pythagoras, so this is a gentle on-ramp. Unit vectors matter because they isolate direction from size, which is exactly what cosine similarity will exploit in a few slides.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
The dot product
The dot product of two vectors multiplies matching components and sums:
\[\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = u_1 v_1 + u_2 v_2 + \dots + u_n v_n\]
Example: \(\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}, \ \mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\) \[\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = 3\cdot1 + 2\cdot4 = 3 + 8 = 11\]

The output is a single number (a scalar), not a vector.
Note: \(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v} = v_1^2 + \dots + v_n^2 = \|\mathbf{v}\|^2\) — the dot product of a vector with itself is its length squared.

u <- c(3, 2); v <- c(1, 4)
sum(u * v)        # 11
u %*% v           # 11 (as a 1x1 matrix)

Define the dot product mechanically first (multiply-and-add) before any geometry. Make them compute the 11 by hand. Two key facts to plant: (1) the result is a scalar, (2) dotting a vector with itself recovers length-squared, linking back to the previous slide. In R both sum(uv) and u %% v work; %% returns a 1x1 matrix, sum(uv) returns a plain number.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
section: What does the dot product mean?
text:
What does the dot product mean?
The geometric identity (this is the key formula of the day):
\[\boxed{\ \mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = \|\mathbf{u}\|\,\|\mathbf{v}\|\cos\theta\ }\]
where \(\theta\) is the angle between the two arrows.

Same direction (\(\theta = 0\)): \(\cos\theta = 1\) → dot product is large & positive.
Right angle (\(\theta = 90^\circ\)): \(\cos\theta = 0\) → dot product is zero.
Opposite (\(\theta = 180^\circ\)): \(\cos\theta = -1\) → large & negative.

So the dot product measures how much two vectors point the same way, scaled by their lengths.

This boxed identity is the conceptual climax. The two definitions — “multiply-and-add” and “lengths times cosine of angle” — are the same number, and that coincidence is what makes linear algebra powerful. Walk through the three cases (aligned, perpendicular, opposite) slowly. The sign of the dot product alone already tells you whether two profiles broadly agree or disagree.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Cosine similarity: comparing two samples
Rearrange the identity to solve for the angle:
\[\cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\|\,\|\mathbf{v}\|}\]
This is cosine similarity — it ranges from \(-1\) to \(1\) and ignores overall magnitude (it only cares about direction).

Why biologists love it: two samples of the same cell type have similar shapes of expression even if one was sequenced twice as deep. Cosine similarity sees them as nearly identical (close to 1); Euclidean distance would be fooled by the depth difference.

cos_sim <- function(u, v) sum(u*v) / (sqrt(sum(u^2)) * sqrt(sum(v^2)))
cos_sim(c(12,0,7), c(6,0,3.5))   # 1  -> same direction, different depth

Cosine similarity is THE payoff of the dot product for omics. Emphasise the depth-invariance: scaling a vector does not change its direction, so cosine similarity is unchanged. That is precisely why it is robust to sequencing-depth differences while raw Euclidean distance is not. The worked R example shows two profiles that differ only by a factor of 2 give cosine similarity exactly 1.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Orthogonality = a right angle
Two vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular) exactly when their dot product is zero:
\[\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = 0 \iff \theta = 90^\circ\]
Example: \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} = 2\cdot0 + 0\cdot5 = 0\). Orthogonal.

Orthogonal directions carry independent information — no overlap.
This idea returns in force when we build PCA: the principal components are orthogonal axes.

sum(c(2,0) * c(0,5))   # 0  -> perpendicular

Orthogonality is just “dot product equals zero”, but it is one of the most reused ideas in the whole course. Flag forward to PCA: principal axes are chosen to be orthogonal precisely so each captures a non-overlapping piece of the variation. For now, the takeaway is the iff: zero dot product is the same statement as right angle.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
section: Projection: the shadow of one vector on another
text:
Projection: the shadow of one vector on another
The projection of \(\mathbf{u}\) onto \(\mathbf{v}\) is the shadow \(\mathbf{u}\) casts on the line through \(\mathbf{v}\) (imagine light coming straight down):
\[\text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u} = \frac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\;\mathbf{v}\]

The fraction \(\dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\) is a scalar: “how many copies of \(\mathbf{v}\)”.
Multiplying by \(\mathbf{v}\) turns that scalar into a vector pointing along \(\mathbf{v}\).
The leftover, \(\mathbf{u} - \text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u}\), is orthogonal to \(\mathbf{v}\).

proj <- function(u, v) (sum(u*v) / sum(v*v)) * v
proj(c(3,3), c(4,0))   # 3 0  -> shadow on the x-axis

Projection is “drop a perpendicular onto the line through v”. Build it in two stages: first the scalar coefficient (how far along v), then re-attach the direction by multiplying by v. The orthogonal-leftover fact is the seed of least-squares regression and Gram–Schmidt later. The R example: projecting (3,3) onto the x-axis vector (4,0) gives (3,0) — the shadow on the floor.

title: Vectors, dot product & projection
text:
Why projection matters biologically

Regression / deconvolution: expressing a sample as “so much of profile A plus so much of profile B” is a sequence of projections.
Removing an unwanted axis: subtract the projection of every sample onto a technical-artifact direction (e.g. a batch effect) to “regress it out”.
PCA preview: projecting all samples onto the top principal axis is exactly what a PCA scatter plot shows.

The dot product, cosine similarity, orthogonality, and projection are all the same machinery viewed from different angles.

Tie the day together. Every later technique in the linear-algebra and PCA blocks is built from these four ideas. Regressing out a batch effect = subtracting a projection; a PCA score = a projection coordinate; comparing cell types = cosine similarity. They are not separate tools, just different uses of the dot product.
